engine.maia: Maia-1 rating-band networks 1100-1900 via zerofish WASM lc0, nodes=1

accuracy.method: mean of volatility-weighted mean and harmonic mean of per-move accuracies (Lichess formulas)

mistakes.title: You had a draw on f7 and never looked for it
mistakes.explanation: Dead lost a moment earlier, you'd just been thrown a lifeline: 28…Ra7 quietly stepped off the defence, and suddenly 29.Bxf7+! saves the whole game. After 29…Kxf7 30.Rf1+ Kg6 31.Qg4+ the black king can never escape the checks — a perpetual, a dead draw, half a point rescued from a −10 position. Instead 29.Qg4 left the bishop asleep on h5 and Black's attack simply rolled on to mate. When you are losing, the saving resource is almost always a check: force the draw before you look at anything else.

takeaways.lesson: When you're losing, hunt for the perpetual check first.
takeaways.detail: Before you accept a lost position, scan every checking move: a king chase that never ends is a draw. Make 'do I have a perpetual?' the very first question whenever the eval is against you.

takeaways.lesson: Your worst-placed piece is often the one that saves you.
takeaways.detail: The bishop had done nothing on h5, yet the whole draw ran through it. When searching for resources, give every idle piece one 'what if it gave check?' look before giving up.

mistakes.title: Grabbed a pawn on h5 while your king burned on c1
mistakes.explanation: Down material with your king on c1 already raked by …Qa5 and the shattered c-file, 26.Bxh5 spent a whole move winning a rook's-pawn on the far edge of the board. Black replied 26…Nxd4!, blowing open the centre: after 27.cxd4 Qa1+ 28.Nb1 the mating net snaps shut. The engine wanted 26.Nb1, dragging pieces back toward the king. Material is worthless if you're the one getting mated — defence comes first.

takeaways.lesson: Don't grab pawns when your own king is under attack.
takeaways.detail: Before any capture, ask whether the move helps your king. A pawn on the far wing won't defend c1. When you're the one being attacked, count defenders, not material.

takeaways.lesson: In a defensive crisis, bring your pieces home.
takeaways.detail: 26.Nb1 looks ugly, but re-routing toward the king is how attacks are survived. On the ropes, prefer the move that adds a defender over the move that wins a pawn.

mistakes.title: b4 instead of b3 — and the a4-knight simply fell
mistakes.explanation: Your knight on a4 was already under fire from the queen on c6 down the c6–b5–a4 diagonal. 17.b3 would have defended it and kept the position glued together; 17.b4 pushed one square too far, left a4 hanging, and 17…Qxa4 just scooped it up. The queen then raided a2 as well, and the game was gone. When a piece is attacked, job one is to keep it defended — check the square before you push the pawn past it.

takeaways.lesson: Don't push a pawn past the square where it defends your own piece.
takeaways.detail: b3 guards a4; b4 doesn't. Before advancing any pawn, check which of your pieces it currently protects — and whether the push abandons them.

takeaways.lesson: Answer threats to your pieces before anything else.
takeaways.detail: After each enemy move, list what is now attacked and whether it's still defended. The queen had hit a4 the move before; the alarm was already ringing.

mistakes.title: Sent the knight to the rim, where it became a target
mistakes.explanation: 12.Na4 heads for the edge with no safe home. From a4 the knight defends nothing and attacks nothing lasting; it just becomes something Black chases with …b6 and …Qc6, and a few moves later it drops off entirely. 12.dxc5 was cleaner, resolving the centre and keeping the game dead level. A knight on a4 or h4 usually helps your opponent more than you — its next move is so often just 'run away.'

takeaways.lesson: Knights belong near the centre, not on the rim.
takeaways.detail: Before playing an Na4/Nh4-type move, ask where the knight goes next. If the honest answer is 'nowhere useful,' choose a central square instead.

takeaways.lesson: Don't create pieces your opponent can attack for free.
takeaways.detail: Every move should add safety or activity. The offside knight did neither and handed Black tempo after tempo until it simply fell.

mistakes.title: The queen went adventuring before the pieces were out
mistakes.explanation: You'd built a genuine edge (about +1.3) with 3.e5 and rapid development. 8.Qg4 lunges at g7 before the bishops are out, and Black just hits back with 8…Qb6, eyeing b2 and d4 and gaining time. The advantage evaporated to equality. 8.Bd3 finishes development and banks the pull instead. Early queen raids usually just hand the opponent free tempi to develop with threats.

takeaways.lesson: Develop your minor pieces before the queen goes hunting.
takeaways.detail: In the opening, ask 'are my bishops and knights out?' before moving the queen a second time. An early queen sortie is usually a gift of tempo to your opponent.

takeaways.lesson: Convert an opening edge by finishing development, not by forcing matters.
takeaways.detail: When you're better, keep improving your worst piece. 8.Bd3 and castling banks the advantage; 8.Qg4 gambles it away for nothing.
